content:
The “fast” part only worked because the slow work already happened: content review, fact-checking, an editorial pipeline that taught us where constraints beat prompting, and an audit that answered a harder question than “which posts need images”—where comprehension actually breaks.

Part 3 of 3 on building the lower dB: how we scaled visual asset generation after locking rules on a single article, why many charts were Matplotlib scripts (consistency at scale beats another pretty render), and how we embedded the workflow in admin with the same human-in-the-loop posture as editing.

If you’re shipping AI-assisted publishing systems, this is the kind of detail that separates demos from production.

How We Produced 79 Visual Assets in a Single Session

By the time I sat down to generate visual assets for 20 articles, I'd already spent weeks in content review, fact-checking, and building out the editorial agent pipeline. I didn't realize it at the time, but that earlier work was the real preparation for this session.

The lower dB had about 20 published articles with no supplemental visuals. For a reader-facing health content site, that's a problem. Visuals aren't decoration. They help readers understand the most complex and most important points in an article. They break up long-form content, reinforce important points, and increase comprehension. For health content in particular, the stakes are higher. A well-placed diagram can be the difference between a reader understanding how their medication works and bouncing to a simpler but less accurate source.

I needed 2-5 assets per article, which meant potentially 100 images. I couldn't do this one at a time in Canva. I needed a system.

The prep work that made the session possible

Before touching any image tool, I did a full content audit of every article on the site. Not "which articles need pictures" but "what are the hardest concepts in each article, and where would a visual actually help a reader understand them?"

For each article, I wrote specific asset recommendations. Not "add an image here" but "a mechanism pathway diagram showing how GLP-1s work from injection to effect" or "a two-column panel showing what evidence is settled vs. what's extrapolated from general research." Each recommendation included the type of visual, what it should contain, and which section of the article it would anchor to.

This audit was informed by all the time I'd already spent inside the editorial pipeline - reviewing content, fact-checking claims, iterating on enrichment flows. I already had a strong sense of what each article's key ideas were, where readers might get lost, and where the density of information was highest. That wasn't extra prep. It was a byproduct of the earlier work.

I also set constraints before generating anything:

  • Two permitted visual styles. Excalidraw sketchy style (black ink, white background, slight line wobble) for technical diagrams, flowcharts, and comparison tables. Woodcut/letterpress style (bold black ink on warm cream parchment) for reference cards, checklists, and anatomical illustrations.

  • A programmatic chart path for anything data-driven - clinical trial results, cost breakdowns, dose timelines, symptom matrices.

  • Editorial guidelines: use reputable sources only, avoid "red flag/green flag" framing, maintain the same clinical objectivity as the articles themselves.

Start with one, iterate, then scale

I chose Manus for the generation session because of its strength with structured content assets. But I didn't start with all 20 articles. I started with one.

The first batch came back close but not right. The legend on a clinical trial bar chart overlapped with the section label. I asked for it moved below the chart and changed from stacked to side-by-side to make better use of the space. A reference card checklist was generated at 16:9 aspect ratio, which made the text too small to read. It needed 4:3. One of the woodcut reference cards included a hand holding the card in a 3D perspective. That wasn't the style.

Each round of feedback became a reusable rule. By the end of the first article, I had documented iteration rules that applied to every future asset:

  • Legends always go below the chart, horizontal, outside the plot area.

  • Reference cards use 4:3 aspect ratio, not 16:9.

  • No hands holding cards, no 3D perspective on flat reference cards.

  • No generic labels like "Event 1" or "Position 1." Every node uses its actual content.

  • Timeline nodes use "[Year]: [Event Name]" format directly. No positional descriptors.

  • Never add directional arrows along the sides of a pyramid diagram. The shape communicates the hierarchy on its own.

Once those rules were locked in, scaling to the remaining articles was fast. The agent needed almost no steering because the constraints were already defined.

Scripts, not just an LLM

Here's the detail that surprises people: most of the data-driven visuals on the site were generated by Python scripts, not by an image model. Clinical trial bar charts, half-life comparisons, cost waterfalls, dose schedule timelines, symptom severity matrices - all generated programmatically with Matplotlib.

The scripts use a standardized boilerplate with the lower dB's brand colors, monospace typography, consistent spine and grid styles, legend placement rules, and footnote formatting all baked in. Every chart that comes out of that boilerplate looks like it belongs on the same site.

That's why those charts are consistent across 20 articles. An image model generating each one independently would have produced 20 slightly different visual styles, even with the same prompt. The LLM's role here was writing the scripts, not drawing the charts. That distinction matters for output quality.

The output

A few hours, 79 reviewed and approved visual assets across the full article library. The asset types ranged widely - mechanism pathway diagrams, comparison tables, flowcharts, decision trees, regulatory timelines, evidence panels, exercise priority pyramids, anatomical illustrations, reference card checklists, and every variety of data chart. Each asset was assigned a layout for placement in its article; either full-width to break the text flow, or inset to float alongside the text with wrapping, depending on whether the visual stands alone or supports the surrounding paragraph.

Every asset has a visual-asset-requirements.md documenting the original requirement, the generation method, and any editorial decisions made during review. That documentation isn't overhead. It's what makes the next round of assets faster.

Systematizing it

At the end of the Manus session, I had the LLM create a comprehensive style guide documenting everything: the brand principles, the prompts that worked for each asset type, the Matplotlib boilerplate, all nine iteration rules, export standards, and a QA checklist. That style guide became the canonical reference for all future visual asset work.

Then we built the workflow into the lower dB's admin system itself. Editors can now propose a visual plan from an approved article draft, review and edit typed visual specs before generation begins, and approve final assets before publication. The generation providers mirror the same split that came out of the Manus session - programmatic_svg for diagrams, cards, timelines, and charts, and openrouter_image for model-backed illustrations.

The visual workflow has the same human-in-the-loop philosophy as the editorial pipeline. Specs are reviewed before generation. Generated assets are reviewed before publication. Nothing goes live without explicit approval.

The compounding effect

The visual asset session was smooth because of everything that came before it. The content review gave me the audit. The editorial pipeline taught me where constraints beat prompt engineering. The enrichment iteration taught me to start narrow and scale only after the first pass was right.

This is the same principle that shows up in building LLM evaluation systems where the upfront work of creating your golden dataset is what determines whether your evaluations are meaningful. Skip the preparation, and the outputs are inconsistent no matter how good the model is. Invest in the preparation, and the generation becomes the easy part. We'll have more to say about evals in a future post.

Whether you're measuring site speed, designing an agent pipeline, or generating 79 images, the preparation and the constraints are the actual product.
seoDescription: How one team generated 79 reviewed visual assets across 20 articles in a single session—using structured prep, style constraints, and programmatic chart generation.
title: 3 Hours to Create 79 Assets. Go.

content:
Building an Editorial AI Pipeline by Narrowing Boundaries

We built a 13-stage editorial AI pipeline that researches, writes, fact-checks, and packages articles for human review. The most important design decisions had nothing to do with prompts.

The first version of the pipeline could produce articles. It just couldn't produce articles that a human reviewer could do anything useful with. The output was a wall of text with no traceability, no way to request a targeted fix, and no clear separation between "the research was bad" and "the writing was bad." That's where the real design work started.

I knew going into this that more specificity at the task level would improve the system. What I didn't know was where it would help most. So instead of over-engineering upfront, I built in rounds of iteration and let the pipeline tell me where additional specificity, guardrails, and structure would make the biggest difference.

What we actually built

The agent team is a research-and-writing system for editorial content on the lower dB. It takes an article brief, runs a durable multi-stage workflow, produces a fact-checked article package, and routes the result through an admin review surface before anything becomes publishable.

The pipeline runs through 13 stages: brief and cost estimate, research plan, source collection, claims analysis, gap-fill search, outline, draft, edit, humanizer pass, fact check, SEO package, CMS bridge, and human review.

One of the earliest and most important decisions was separating four concerns that would have been easy to collapse into one system:

  • Execution: Trigger.dev owns the long-running orchestration, retries, and fan-out.

  • Artifacts: Every stage writes its output to a canonical file store. Research plans, claims tables, search results, outlines, drafts, fact checks, usage ledgers. All inspectable, all durable.

  • Operator state: The admin UI handles job queues, review decisions, and rerun requests.

  • Observability: Arize captures traces and prompt metadata.

That split meant we could iterate on prompts, reruns, and review workflows without turning the workflow engine, the UI, and the storage model into one inseparable system. In plain terms - changing how we write articles doesn't require changing how we review them, and vice versa.

Review is a state machine, not an approval button

The first version of human review was a single approve/reject decision at the end of the pipeline. That broke down fast.

If a reviewer saw a problem in the outline but the draft was otherwise good, there was no way to rerun just the outline and everything downstream. The only option was to rerun the entire job. That's expensive, slow, and makes review feel careless.

We added stage-targeted replay. A reviewer can now pick a stage and rerun from there. The skipped stages inherit their artifacts from the previous job. Source gathering still runs fresh. Preflight checks verify that inherited artifacts exist before execution starts. Each rerun creates a self-contained job with full traceability, so you can always trace what changed and where the rerun started.

This matters because human review happens throughout the process, not just at the end. The system is designed for a reviewer to catch a factual gap, request a targeted fix, and get a revised package back without paying to rebuild the entire research chain. That's what human-in-the-loop actually means in practice: not a rubber stamp after generation, but a feedback channel at every stage that matters.

Factual grounding needed a search stage, not a bigger prompt

The original research path was good at synthesizing sources the system already had. It was weaker when the initial source set missed a specific provider fact or policy detail.

The first instinct might be to add "also go find anything missing" to the writer or claims prompt. We tried something narrower instead. We added a dedicated gap-fill search stage between claims analysis and outline. After the pipeline extracts claims from the source material, a model reviews the claims table and identifies what's still unverified. It generates up to five targeted search queries, runs them through Tavily in parallel, and reruns claims analysis with the added evidence.

The gap-fill stage was designed to fail gracefully. If some searches return nothing useful, the pipeline continues with what it has rather than stalling the whole article. That resilience turned out to matter as much as the coverage improvement.

The lesson - search is more reliable when driven by explicit claim gaps than by a broad "research more" instruction. A narrow, well-placed stage beat a smarter prompt.

Enrichment became its own editorial pipeline

After the main article pipeline was working, we needed publication metadata like summaries, description lines, tags, key points, related articles. The first version was a single prompt that generated everything at once.

That was too coarse. Reviewers wanted to approve the summary but regenerate the tags. Or lock the key points but tweak the description. A single-prompt approach made every rerun all-or-nothing.

We split enrichment into focused tasks: summary, description, tags/category, and key points, each generated separately. Fields that could be computed deterministically stayed out of the model path entirely. Read time, citations, and related article rankings are all calculated by code, not generated by a prompt.

Reviewers can now lock individual fields and request field-targeted reruns with specific guidance. Approved fields survive reruns. Each generation run carries provenance, so you can compare outputs across versions and build evaluation sets later.

That shift was a recurring pattern - the system got more deterministic over time, and that was a feature, not a retreat from AI. Style guardrails, related-article selection, and citation handling all became more reliable when they moved out of prompt-only control and into code.

Production forced better infrastructure

The jump from local iteration to production deployment exposed every weak assumption.

When we needed to migrate existing articles to the new enrichment system, a one-shot script wasn't good enough. Partial failures needed explicit handling. Reruns couldn't depend on keeping a browser tab open. UI state and background task state had to stay aligned even when jobs ran for minutes.

We moved enrichment onto Trigger-backed durable tasks with persisted batch and item records. Each backfill run is auditable, each item has explicit settlement status, and the system can recover from interruptions without duplicating work. The answer wasn't to make the prompts more reliable. It was to make the workflow infrastructure take responsibility for durability.

What the iteration taught us

The implementation history is mostly a story of narrowing things down:

  • Narrower workflow responsibilities. Each stage does one job and writes one artifact.

  • Narrower rerun scopes. Reviewers fix what's wrong without rebuilding what's right.

  • Narrower prompt responsibilities. Anything that can be computed deterministically should be.

  • Narrower but stronger deterministic rules. Style guardrails enforced by code catch things that prompt instructions miss inconsistently.

The pipeline got better every time it got more explicit about state, lineage, and ownership. Not when we wrote cleverer prompts.

If there's one transferable idea here, it's this - treat AI generation as a durable, inspectable editorial workflow, not as one large prompt behind a button. The prompts matter, but the boundaries, the artifacts, the review state, and the feedback loops matter more.
seoDescription: How we built a 13-stage editorial AI pipeline and why the biggest gains came from narrowing boundaries, not writing better prompts.
title: Building a Robust, Redundant Editorial Agent Team
